title: Causal Inference Methods – Worked Examples
text:
This supplement provides four worked examples of design-based causal inference methods frequently used in science and education research: propensity score weighting, regression discontinuity, difference-in-differences, and instrumental variables.
Each example illustrates the logic of identification, the assumptions required for causal interpretation, and standard diagnostic and robustness checks. Sensitivity analysis accompanies each design to assess how strongly conclusions depend on unverifiable assumptions.
The aim is to clarify how different research designs support causal claims, and to demonstrate how methodological transparency strengthens statistical conclusions.

title: Causal Inference Methods – Worked Examples
text:
Included Methods

1. Propensity Score Weighting (CBPS)
A full example of inverse probability of treatment weighting using Covariate Balancing Propensity Scores (CBPS), including:

Construction of analytic variables
Balance diagnostics (before and after weighting)
Weighted outcome regression
Sensitivity analysis using konfound

👉 Open the example:
Appendix A1 – Propensity Score Weighting


2. Regression Discontinuity (Sharp RD)
A simulated sharp RD design demonstrating:

Bandwidth selection (MSE-optimal)
RD estimation with and without covariates
Density / manipulation tests
Covariate balance checks
Bandwidth sensitivity
Sensitivity analysis with konfound

👉 Open the example:
Appendix A2 – Regression Discontinuity


3. Difference-in-Differences (Two-Period DiD)
A two-wave DiD example using school-level treatment adoption:

Sample construction
Long-format panel restructuring
Fixed-effects estimation (fixest)
Visual trend inspection
Sensitivity analysis with konfound

👉 Open the example:
Appendix A3 – Difference-in-Differences


4. Instrumental Variables (2SLS)
A simulated IV example illustrating:

Endogeneity from unobserved confounding
Two-stage least squares estimation
First-stage diagnostics
Sensitivity analysis with konfound

👉 Open the example:
Appendix A4 – Instrumental Variables

title: Causal Inference Methods – Worked Examples
text:
Notes on Reproducibility

All examples are written as independent .qmd files.
Required packages are loaded within each file.
Rendered HTML documents are included for convenience.
Supporting helper file: rdplot_function.R (used in the RDD example).

To re-render any example:
quarto render appendix-a1-psm.qmd
quarto render appendix-a2-rdd.qmd
quarto render appendix-a3-did.qmd
quarto render appendix-a4-iv.qmd

title: Appendix A3. Difference-in-Differences (DiD) Example
text: This appendix illustrates a simple two-period DiD setup using HSLS variables. The outcome is science self-efficacy measured at baseline (X1SCIEFF) and follow-up (X2SCIEFF). Schools are treated if they adopt a PD requirement by follow-up (while having no requirement at baseline). We estimate a baseline DiD and then a version that adds student covariates for precision.

section: A3.4 Recode labeled missing categories as NA
text:
A3.4 Recode labeled missing categories as NA
HSLS includes several labeled categories that essentially mean “missing” or “not applicable”.
For this example, we drop them by recoding to NA.


Code
missing_levels <- c(
  "Item legitimate skip/NA",
  "Unit non-response",
  "Missing",
  "Item not administered: abbreviated interview",
  "Component not applicable"
)

vars_clean <- vars_needed %>%
  mutate(across(
    .cols = c(A1MSPDLEARN, A2MSPDLEARN,
              X1SEX, X1RACE, X1DUALLANG, X1LOCALE, X1SESQ5),
    .fns = ~ ifelse(as.character(.x) %in% missing_levels, NA, as.character(.x))
  ))

title: Appendix A3. Difference-in-Differences (DiD) Example
section: A3.5 Construct treatment indicators and covariates
text:
A3.5 Construct treatment indicators and covariates
Here a1 and a2 indicate whether a PD requirement is present at baseline vs follow-up.
We also create a compact set of student-level covariates.


Code
analytic <- vars_clean %>%
  mutate(
    a1 = if_else(A1MSPDLEARN == "Yes", 1, 0, missing = NA),
    a2 = if_else(A2MSPDLEARN == "Yes", 1, 0, missing = NA),

    female = if_else(X1SEX == "Female", 1, 0, missing = NA),

    race_white = if_else(X1RACE == "White, non-Hispanic", 1, 0, missing = NA),
    race_black = if_else(X1RACE == "Black/African-American, non-Hispanic", 1, 0, missing = NA),
    race_asian = if_else(X1RACE == " Asian, non-Hispanic ", 1, 0, missing = NA),

    dual_lang = if_else(X1DUALLANG == "First language is English only", 1, 0, missing = NA),
    locale_rural = if_else(X1LOCALE == "Rural", 1, 0, missing = NA)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    SES = factor(
      X1SESQ5,
      levels = c(
        "First quintile (lowest)",
        "Second quintile",
        "Third quintile",
        "Fourth quintile",
        "Fifth quintile (highest)"
      ),
      labels = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
    ) %>% as.numeric()
  )

title: Appendix A3. Difference-in-Differences (DiD) Example
section: A3.7 Define treated vs never-treated schools (based on adoption)
text:
A3.7 Define treated vs never-treated schools (based on adoption)
We restrict to schools with no requirement at baseline (a1 == 0).
Then:

treated schools: adopt by follow-up (a2 == 1)
control schools: never adopt (a2 == 0)



Code
hsls_did <- analytic_clean %>%
  filter(a1 == 0) %>%
  mutate(
    treat_school  = ifelse(a1 == 0 & a2 == 1, 1, 0),
    never_treated = ifelse(a1 == 0 & a2 == 0, 1, 0)
  ) %>%
  filter(treat_school == 1 | never_treated == 1)

table(hsls_did$treat_school)



   0    1 
3546 2810

title: Appendix A3. Difference-in-Differences (DiD) Example
section: A3.8 Simulate school IDs for clustering (SCH_ID_SIM)
text:
A3.8 Simulate school IDs for clustering (SCH_ID_SIM)
In a proper DiD, you normally already have a stable school identifier for clustering.
Here we simulate a school ID assignment to keep the example self-contained (and to show the mechanics of clustering in fixest).


Code
# Student counts by adoption status at follow-up
n_treatment_students <- sum(hsls_did$a2 == 1, na.rm = TRUE)
n_control_students   <- sum(hsls_did$a2 == 0, na.rm = TRUE)

# Allocate schools proportionally (tweak if you want)
n_total_schools <- 80
prop_treatment <- n_treatment_students / (n_treatment_students + n_control_students)

n_schools_treatment <- round(n_total_schools * prop_treatment)
n_schools_control   <- n_total_schools - n_schools_treatment

# Assign simulated school IDs
hsls_did$SCH_ID_SIM <- NA_integer_

idx_treatment <- hsls_did$a2 == 1
hsls_did$SCH_ID_SIM[idx_treatment] <-
  sample(1:n_schools_treatment, sum(idx_treatment), replace = TRUE)

idx_control <- hsls_did$a2 == 0
hsls_did$SCH_ID_SIM[idx_control] <-
  sample((n_schools_treatment + 1):(n_schools_treatment + n_schools_control),
         sum(idx_control), replace = TRUE)

table(hsls_did$SCH_ID_SIM)[1:10]

title: Appendix A3. Difference-in-Differences (DiD) Example
section: A3.9 Reshape to long format (two rows per student)
text:
A3.9 Reshape to long format (two rows per student)
We stack baseline and follow-up outcomes, then create:

post: 0 baseline, 1 follow-up
treat_post: DiD interaction term



Code
cols_to_keep <- c(
  "STU_ID", "SCH_ID_SIM", "treat_school",
  "X1SCIEFF", "X2SCIEFF",
  "female", "race_white", "race_black", "race_asian",
  "dual_lang", "locale_rural", "SES"
)

hsls_long <- hsls_did[, cols_to_keep] %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols      = c(X1SCIEFF, X2SCIEFF),
    names_to  = "wave",
    values_to = "SCI_SE"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    post       = ifelse(wave == "X2SCIEFF", 1, 0),
    treat_post = treat_school * post
  )

table(hsls_long$post)



   0    1 
6356 6356

title: Appendix A3. Difference-in-Differences (DiD) Example
section: A3.10 DiD estimation (two-way setup with school fixed effects)
text:
A3.10 DiD estimation (two-way setup with school fixed effects)

(a) Basic DiD
This is the clean baseline model: outcome on post and treat_post, with school fixed effects.


Code
did_basic <- feols(
  SCI_SE ~ post + treat_post | SCH_ID_SIM,
  data = hsls_long
)

summary(did_basic)

(b) DiD with student covariates (precision controls)
Adding covariates is not required for identification in DiD, but it can help precision and guard against small sample quirks.


Code
did_cov <- feols(
  SCI_SE ~ post + treat_post +
    female + race_white + race_black + race_asian +
    dual_lang + locale_rural + SES | SCH_ID_SIM,
  data = hsls_long
)

title: Appendix A3. Difference-in-Differences (DiD) Example
section: A3.11 Mean trends plot (treated vs control)
text:
A3.11 Mean trends plot (treated vs control)
This plot is a quick visual check of how treated and control groups move from baseline to follow-up.
With only two time points, we can’t really “test” parallel trends, but at least we can see whether the groups are moving in obviously different ways.


Code
trend_dat <- hsls_long %>%
  group_by(treat_school, post) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_SCI_SE = mean(SCI_SE, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(
  trend_dat,
  aes(
    x = post,
    y = mean_SCI_SE,
    group = factor(treat_school),
    linetype = factor(treat_school),
    shape = factor(treat_school)
  )
) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0, 1), labels = c("Baseline", "Follow-up")) +
  labs(
    x = "Wave",
    y = "Mean science self-efficacy",
    linetype = "Treated school",
    shape = "Treated school",
    caption = "Dashed line marks the intervention period."
  ) +
  theme_minimal()





Mean science self-efficacy over time by treatment group.

title: Appendix A3. Difference-in-Differences (DiD) Example
text:
A3.12 Sensitivity analysis (Konfound)
Below we run konfound using the estimate and SE you provided for the DiD effect.


Code
pkonfound(
  est_eff      = -0.0346,
  std_err      = 0.1025,
  n_obs        = 6356,
  n_covariates = 2,
  index        = "RIR"
)


Robustness of Inference to Replacement (RIR):
RIR = 5262

The estimated effect is -0.035. The threshold value for statistical significance
is -0.201 (with null hypothesis = 0 and alpha = 0.05). To reach that threshold,
82.78% of the estimate of -0.035 would have to be due to bias. This implies to sustain
an inference one would expect to have to replace 5262 (82.78%) observations with
effect of 0 with data points with effect of -0.201 (RIR = 5262).

See Frank et al. (2013) for a description of the method.

Citation: Frank, K.A., Maroulis, S., Duong, M., and Kelcey, B. (2013).
What would it take to change an inference?
Using Rubin's causal model to interpret the robustness of causal inferences.
Education, Evaluation and Policy Analysis, 35 437-460.

Accuracy of results increases with the number of decimals reported.


Code
pkonfound(
  est_eff      = -0.0346,
  std_err      = 0.1025,
  n_obs        = 6356,
  n_covariates = 2,
  index        = "IT"
)


Impact Threshold for a Confounding Variable (ITCV):
The maximum impact of an omitted variable to sustain an inference for a null hypothesis
of an effect of 0 (nu) is based on a correlation of 0.141 with the outcome and 0.141
with the predictor of interest (conditioning on all observed covariates in the model;
signs are interchangeable if they are different). This is based on a threshold effect of
-0.201 for statistical significance (alpha = 0.05).

Correspondingly the maximum impact of an omitted variable (as defined in Frank 2000) is 
0.141 X 0.141 = 0.02 to sustain an inference for a null hypothesis of an effect of 0 (nu).

For calculation of unconditional ITCV using pkonfound(), additionally include
the R2, sdx, and sdy as input.

See Frank (2000) for a description of the method.

Citation:
Frank, K. (2000). Impact of a confounding variable on the inference of a
regression coefficient. Sociological Methods and Research, 29 (2), 147-194

Accuracy of results increases with the number of decimals reported.

The ITCV analysis was originally derived for OLS standard errors. If the
standard errors reported in the table were not based on OLS, some caution
should be used to interpret the ITCV.

title: Appendix A1. Propensity Score Weighting (CBPS-IPTW)
text: This appendix example estimates the average treatment effect (ATE) of having an IEP on science self-efficacy (X1SCIEFF). We (1) build an analytic dataset, (2) estimate propensity score weights using CBPS, (3) check covariate balance, (4) fit a weighted outcome model, and (5) run a sensitivity analysis with konfound.

title: Appendix A1. Propensity Score Weighting (CBPS-IPTW)

text:
A1.4 Recode missing categories as NA
Some HSLS variables code missingness as labeled categories.
Here we convert those labels to NA so that downstream modeling behaves as expected.


Code
missing_levels <- c(
  "Item legitimate skip/NA",
  "Unit non-response",
  "Missing"
)

vars_clean <- vars_needed %>%
  mutate(across(
    .cols = c(X1IEPFLAG, X1SEX, X1RACE, X1DUALLANG, X1LOCALE,
              X1SESQ5, P1MARSTAT, P1HIDEG1, P1HIDEG2, P1SLD, P1DD),
    .fns = ~ ifelse(as.character(.x) %in% missing_levels, NA, as.character(.x))
  ))

title: Appendix A1. Propensity Score Weighting (CBPS-IPTW)
text:
A1.5 Construct analysis variables (treatment + covariates)
The goal here is not to be clever—just to turn a handful of key survey fields into clean indicators that are easy to use in the weighting model.


Code
analytic <- vars_clean %>%
  mutate(
    # treatment: IEP
    IEP = if_else(X1IEPFLAG == "Student has an IEP", 1, 0, missing = NA),

    # demographics
    female = if_else(X1SEX == "Female", 1, 0, missing = NA),

    race_white = if_else(X1RACE == "White, non-Hispanic", 1, 0, missing = NA),
    race_black = if_else(X1RACE == "Black/African-American, non-Hispanic", 1, 0, missing = NA),
    race_asian = if_else(X1RACE == " Asian, non-Hispanic ", 1, 0, missing = NA),
    race_hispanic = if_else(X1RACE == " Hispanic, race specified ", 1, 0, missing = NA),

    # language / locale
    dual_lang = if_else(X1DUALLANG == "First language is English only", 1, 0, missing = NA),
    locale_rural = if_else(X1LOCALE == "Rural", 1, 0, missing = NA),

    # family background
    divorced = if_else(P1MARSTAT %in% c("Divorced", "Separated"), 1, 0, missing = NA),
    bachelorabove1 = if_else(P1HIDEG1 %in% c("Bachelor's degree", "Master's degree"), 1, 0, missing = NA),
    bachelorabove2 = if_else(P1HIDEG2 %in% c("Bachelor's degree", "Master's degree"), 1, 0, missing = NA),

    # parent-reported conditions
    LD = if_else(P1SLD == "Yes", 1, 0, missing = NA),
    DD = if_else(P1DD == "Yes", 1, 0, missing = NA)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    # SES: convert ordered quintiles into a simple numeric scale 1–5
    SES = factor(
      X1SESQ5,
      levels = c(
        "First quintile (lowest)",
        "Second quintile",
        "Third quintile",
        "Fourth quintile",
        "Fifth quintile (highest)"
      ),
      labels = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
    ) %>% as.numeric()
  )

title: Appendix A1. Propensity Score Weighting (CBPS-IPTW)
text:
A1.7 Estimate CBPS weights (ATE)
We use CBPS to estimate weights that balance covariates by construction.
Then we normalize the weights so the average weight is 1 (helps with interpretability and avoids tiny/huge totals).


Code
cbps_fit <- CBPS(
  IEP ~ female + race_white + race_black +
    dual_lang + locale_rural + SES + divorced +
    bachelorabove1 + bachelorabove2 + LD + DD,
  data = analytic_clean,
  method = "exact",
  ATT = 0
)

analytic_clean$IPTW <- cbps_fit$weights
summary(analytic_clean$IPTW)

0.0001961 0.0002028 0.0002079 0.0003786 0.0002191 0.0052690 


Code
# Normalize weights: mean(weight) = 1
analytic_clean$finalWeightATE <- with(analytic_clean, IPTW / mean(IPTW))

with(analytic_clean, by(finalWeightATE, IEP, summary))

title: Appendix A1. Propensity Score Weighting (CBPS-IPTW)
text:
A1.10 Sensitivity analysis (Konfound)
Here we run konfound using the reported estimate and standard error.
In practice, you’ll usually pull the estimate/SE directly from the fitted model output, but the hard-coded values are fine for a fixed appendix example.


Code
# RIR index
pkonfound(
  est_eff = -0.63613,
  std_err = 0.16460,
  n_obs = 5282,
  n_covariates = 1,
  index = "RIR"
)


Robustness of Inference to Replacement (RIR):
RIR = 2603

To nullify the inference of an effect using the threshold of -0.323 for
statistical significance (with null hypothesis = 0 and alpha = 0.05), 49.274%
of the estimate of -0.636 would have to be due to bias. This implies that to
nullify the inference one would expect to have to replace 2603 (49.274%)
observations with data points for which the effect is 0 (RIR = 2603).

See Frank et al. (2013) for a description of the method.

Citation: Frank, K.A., Maroulis, S., Duong, M., and Kelcey, B. (2013).
What would it take to change an inference?
Using Rubin's causal model to interpret the robustness of causal inferences.
Education, Evaluation and Policy Analysis, 35 437-460.

Accuracy of results increases with the number of decimals reported.


Code
# IT index
pkonfound(
  est_eff = -0.63613,
  std_err = 0.16460,
  n_obs = 5282,
  n_covariates = 1,
  index = "IT"
)


Impact Threshold for a Confounding Variable (ITCV):
The minimum (in absolute value) impact of an omitted variable to nullify an inference
for a null hypothesis of an effect of 0 (nu) is based on a correlation of -0.164 with the
outcome and 0.164 with the predictor of interest (conditioning on all observed covariates
in the model; signs are interchangeable if they are different). This is based on a threshold
effect of -0.027 for statistical significance (alpha = 0.05).

Correspondingly the conditional impact of an omitted variable (as defined in Frank 2000) must be
-0.164 X 0.164 = -0.027 to nullify an inference for a null hypothesis of an effect of 0 (nu).

For calculation of unconditional ITCV using pkonfound(), additionally include
the R2, sdx, and sdy as input.

See Frank (2000) for a description of the method.

Citation:
Frank, K. (2000). Impact of a confounding variable on the inference of a
regression coefficient. Sociological Methods and Research, 29 (2), 147-194

Accuracy of results increases with the number of decimals reported.

The ITCV analysis was originally derived for OLS standard errors. If the
standard errors reported in the table were not based on OLS, some caution
should be used to interpret the ITCV.

title: Appendix A2. Regression Discontinuity (Sharp RD) Example
text:
This appendix walks through a simple sharp regression discontinuity design (RDD) using simulated data.
The running variable is x with a cutoff at 50, treatment is assigned deterministically (D = 1[x ≥ 50]), and the outcome y has a true jump at the cutoff.
We follow a basic RDD workflow: 1) simulate data,
2) pick a bandwidth,
3) estimate the discontinuity (with and without covariates),
4) run a few standard checks (density/manipulation; covariate balance),
5) do a quick bandwidth sensitivity check, and
6) run konfound on the main estimate.

title: Appendix A2. Regression Discontinuity (Sharp RD) Example
text:
This appendix walks through a simple sharp regression discontinuity design (RDD) using simulated data.
The running variable is x with a cutoff at 50, treatment is assigned deterministically (D = 1[x ≥ 50]), and the outcome y has a true jump at the cutoff.
We follow a basic RDD workflow: 1) simulate data,
2) pick a bandwidth,
3) estimate the discontinuity (with and without covariates),
4) run a few standard checks (density/manipulation; covariate balance),
5) do a quick bandwidth sensitivity check, and
6) run konfound on the main estimate.

title: Appendix A2. Regression Discontinuity (Sharp RD) Example
text:
A2.1 Setup


Code
set.seed(5)

# General data and plotting
library(tidyverse)

# RDD estimation
library(rdrobust)
library(rddensity)  # Cattaneo et al. density test + plot

# Sensitivity analysis
library(konfound)

# Custom plotting helper used later (expects file to be in the working directory)
source("rdplot_function.R")

title: Appendix A2. Regression Discontinuity (Sharp RD) Example
section: A2.2 Simulate a sharp RD dataset
text:
A2.2 Simulate a sharp RD dataset
A few small implementation choices here are just to keep things tidy:

We cap the running variable to [0, 100] to avoid extreme tails.
Covariates are generated independently of treatment assignment (so balance should hold around the cutoff).



Code
n        <- 5000
cutoff   <- 50
true_tau <- 40  # true local treatment effect (jump at the cutoff)

dat <- tibble(
  x = rnorm(n, 50, 25)
) %>%
  mutate(
    x = if_else(x < 0, 0, x),
    x = if_else(x > 100, 100, x),
    D = if_else(x >= cutoff, 1, 0),  # sharp assignment
    ses          = rnorm(n(), 0, 1),
    female       = rbinom(n(), 1, 0.5),
    sci_interest = rnorm(n(), 0, 1)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    y = 25 +
      true_tau * D +     # discontinuity
      1.5 * x +          # smooth relationship with x
      5   * ses +
      3   * female +
      4   * sci_interest +
      rnorm(n(), 0, 20)
  )

glimpse(dat)

Columns: 6

title: Appendix A2. Regression Discontinuity (Sharp RD) Example
section: A2.3 Quick plot (does the jump look plausible?)
text:
A2.3 Quick plot (does the jump look plausible?)
This is not “proof” of anything, but it’s a good first check.
If the plot looks flat or noisy right at the cutoff, it’s worth slowing down before trusting estimates.


Code
ggplot(dat, aes(x, y, colour = factor(D))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = cutoff, colour = "grey40", linetype = 2) +
  stat_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    x = "Running variable (X)",
    y = "Outcome (Y)",
    colour = "Treatment (D)"
  )





Raw scatter plot with a vertical line at the cutoff (50).

title: Appendix A2. Regression Discontinuity (Sharp RD) Example
text:
A2.4 Bandwidth selection (triangular kernel)
Here we use rdbwselect() and take the main MSE-optimal bandwidth.
This bandwidth is the default starting point for the main estimate.


Code
bw_tri <- rdbwselect(
  y      = dat$y,
  x      = dat$x,
  c      = cutoff,
  kernel = "triangular"
)

# Main MSE-optimal bandwidth (first row/column in the bws matrix)
main_bw <- bw_tri$bws[1, 1]
main_bw


[1] 11.79141

title: Appendix A2. Regression Discontinuity (Sharp RD) Example

A2.5 Main RD estimate (with and without covariates)

Simple RD: outcome on running variable only.
Covariate-adjusted RD: adds covariates as precision controls (should not change identification in sharp RD).

  y      = dat$y,
  x      = dat$x,
  c      = cutoff,
  h      = main_bw,
  kernel = "triangular"

text:
A2.6 Manipulation / density checks around the cutoff
A common RDD worry is that people can “sort” around the cutoff (i.e., manipulate the running variable).
Density discontinuity tests are one way to flag that kind of problem.

  c          = cutoff,
  h          = main_bw,
  vce        = "jackknife",

)

title: Appendix A2. Regression Discontinuity (Sharp RD) Example
section: A2.7 Covariate balance near the cutoff
text:
A2.7 Covariate balance near the cutoff
If RDD is behaving as expected, covariates should be smooth at the cutoff.
This isn’t a formal proof, but obvious jumps in pre-treatment covariates are a red flag.


Code
dat1 <- dat %>%
  mutate(x_centered = x - cutoff)




Code
plot.RD(
  female ~ x_centered,
  treat  = "D",
  data   = dat1,
  yaxt   = "n",
  linecol = "black",
  kdens  = "rectangular",
  h      = main_bw,
  cex    = 0.5,
  main   = "Covariate Balance Check: female",
  ylab   = "female",
  xlab   = "Running Variable (Centered)",
  xlim   = c(-50, 50)
)
axis(side = 2, at = c(0, 1), labels = c(0, 1))





Covariate balance check at the cutoff: female.






Code
plot.RD(
  ses ~ x_centered,
  treat  = "D",
  data   = dat1,
  linecol = "black",
  kdens  = "rectangular",
  h      = main_bw,
  cex    = 0.5,
  main   = "Covariate Balance Check: SES",

title: Appendix A2. Regression Discontinuity (Sharp RD) Example
text:
A2.9 Sensitivity analysis (Konfound)
Here we run konfound on the estimate/SE you provided.
If you want this to be fully automatic, we can extract the estimate and SE from rd_main_simple instead of hard-coding them.


Code
pkonfound(
  est_eff      = 38.943,
  std_err      = 11.011,
  n_obs        = 1788,
  n_covariates = 1,
  index        = "IT"
)


Impact Threshold for a Confounding Variable (ITCV):
The minimum impact of an omitted variable to nullify an inference for
a null hypothesis of an effect of 0 (nu) is based on a correlation of 0.197
with the outcome and 0.197 with the predictor of interest (conditioning on all
observed covariates in the model; signs are interchangeable if they are different).
This is based on a threshold effect of 0.046 for statistical significance (alpha = 0.05).

Correspondingly the conditional impact of an omitted variable (as defined in Frank 2000) must be 
0.197 X 0.197 = 0.039 to nullify an inference for a null hypothesis of an effect of 0 (nu).

For calculation of unconditional ITCV using pkonfound(), additionally include
the R2, sdx, and sdy as input.

See Frank (2000) for a description of the method.

Citation:
Frank, K. (2000). Impact of a confounding variable on the inference of a
regression coefficient. Sociological Methods and Research, 29 (2), 147-194

Accuracy of results increases with the number of decimals reported.

The ITCV analysis was originally derived for OLS standard errors. If the
standard errors reported in the table were not based on OLS, some caution
should be used to interpret the ITCV.


Code
pkonfound(
  est_eff      = 38.943,
  std_err      = 11.011,
  n_obs        = 1788,
  n_covariates = 1,
  index        = "RIR"
)


Robustness of Inference to Replacement (RIR):
RIR = 796

To nullify the inference of an effect using the threshold of 21.596 for
statistical significance (with null hypothesis = 0 and alpha = 0.05), 44.545%
of the estimate of 38.943 would have to be due to bias. This implies that to
nullify the inference one would expect to have to replace 796 (44.545%)
observations with data points for which the effect is 0 (RIR = 796).

See Frank et al. (2013) for a description of the method.

Citation: Frank, K.A., Maroulis, S., Duong, M., and Kelcey, B. (2013).
What would it take to change an inference?
Using Rubin's causal model to interpret the robustness of causal inferences.
Education, Evaluation and Policy Analysis, 35 437-460.

Accuracy of results increases with the number of decimals reported.

title: Appendix A4. Instrumental Variables (IV / 2SLS) Example
text:
This appendix gives a small IV example using simulated data.
We generate a setting where the treatment (friends, having friends in the classroom) is confounded by an unobserved factor (motivation).
A plausible instrument (distance, minutes to school) affects the treatment but does not directly affect test scores except through treatment.
We then estimate the effect of friends on scores using 2SLS and run a konfound sensitivity analysis on the IV estimate.

title: Appendix A4. Instrumental Variables (IV / 2SLS) Example
text:
This appendix gives a small IV example using simulated data.
We generate a setting where the treatment (friends, having friends in the classroom) is confounded by an unobserved factor (motivation).
A plausible instrument (distance, minutes to school) affects the treatment but does not directly affect test scores except through treatment.
We then estimate the effect of friends on scores using 2SLS and run a konfound sensitivity analysis on the IV estimate.

title: Appendix A4. Instrumental Variables (IV / 2SLS) Example
text:
A4.1 Setup


Code
set.seed(123)

library(AER)      # provides ivreg()
library(konfound)
library(dplyr)

title: Appendix A4. Instrumental Variables (IV / 2SLS) Example
text:
A4.2 Simulate data
A quick note on the simulation logic:

motivation affects both friends and scores (this is the confounder).
distance shifts the likelihood of friends but does not enter the outcome equation directly.



Code
n <- 2000

# Instrument: distance to school (minutes)
distance <- rnorm(n, mean = 20, sd = 8)

# Unobserved confounder: student motivation
motivation <- rnorm(n)

# Treatment: having friends in the classroom (binary)
prob_friends <- plogis(-0.5 + 0.08 * distance - 0.5 * motivation)
friends <- rbinom(n, 1, prob_friends)

# Outcome: test scores (friends has a true causal effect of +3)
scores <- 50 + 3 * friends + 5 * motivation + rnorm(n, sd = 5)

dat <- data.frame(scores, friends, distance, motivation)

summary(dat)
